name: Narrative Engagement Scale / Transportation Scale / Imaginative Engagement Scale (IES)
core_claim: This cluster of validated self-report instruments claims that what drives narrative enjoyment is not WHAT a story is about but the QUALITY and DEPTH of the experiential/cognitive process a viewer undergoes while engaging with it. The named subscales in the brief — Coauthoring, Gap-Filling, Theory of Mind, Reflection — belong specifically to the Imaginative Engagement Scale (IES; Black, Ruedinger & Barnes, 2021, Media Psychology), a TRAIT measure of an individual's habitual tendency to step back, imagine, and think while reading/watching fiction. Its sibling instruments make the same family of claims at different levels: the Narrative Engagement Measure (NEM; Busselle & Bilandzic, 2009) measures STATE engagement with a particular story across four dimensions (narrative understanding, attentional focus, narrative presence, emotional engagement), and the Transportation Scale (Green & Brock, 2000; covered in detail in the sibling 'narrative-transportation' item) measures absorptive immersion. The central, repeatedly-validated empirical claim across all three is that higher engagement on these dimensions predicts higher self-reported ENJOYMENT, more positive story ratings, stronger story-consistent attitude change, and corroborating psychophysiological markers. The IES's specific contribution is to add the COGNITIVE/REFLECTIVE side of engagement (you actively co-build the story, fill its gaps, model characters' minds, and reflect on meaning) that absorption-only constructs like transportation miss — and to show this predicts story rating and state transportation over and above trait transportability and need for cognition.
descriptive_vs_experiential: Squarely EXPERIENTIAL, not descriptive — none of these instruments say anything about a story's subject matter, genre, or theme; they measure the felt and cognitive PROCESS of engaging. Within the experiential space they split across two sub-levels. The NEM and Transportation Scale measure the AFFECTIVE/ABSORPTIVE quality of a specific engagement episode (how present, attentive, emotionally connected, immersed the viewer was). The IES is different and is the reason this item is distinct from the transportation item: it is a COGNITIVE-engagement measure, capturing effortful, reflective, world-building mental activity (anticipating and co-creating plot, inferring across narrative gaps, theorising about characters' minds, reflecting on themes). So the cluster spans an affective-immersion axis (transportation/presence/emotional engagement) AND a cognitive-elaboration axis (coauthoring/gap-filling/ToM/reflection). Both are orthogonal to HelpME2C's topical theme vocabulary. Crucially, the IES and Transportability are TRAIT (person-level) measures; the NEM and Transportation Scale are STATE (episode-level) measures — a distinction that matters more here than the descriptive/experiential split.
key_dimensions: Imaginative Engagement Scale (IES; Black, Ruedinger & Barnes, 2021) — four trait subscales: (1) COAUTHORING — the tendency to mentally contribute to and anticipate the story's direction, imaginatively extending or co-creating the narrative beyond what is on the page/screen; (2) GAP-FILLING — actively making inferences to resolve narrative ambiguity and bridge what the text leaves unstated; (3) THEORY OF MIND — puzzling over and tracking characters' motivations, emotions, and mental states; (4) REFLECTION — contemplating the story's themes and their broader or personally-relevant meaning. The IES is a Likert trait scale and was shown to predict story rating and state transportation over and above trait Transportability and to relate more strongly to Need for Cognition than prior engagement measures. Narrative Engagement Measure (NEM; Busselle & Bilandzic, 2009) — four state subscales: (a) NARRATIVE UNDERSTANDING (ease of making sense of the story), (b) ATTENTIONAL FOCUS (undistracted attention on story events), (c) NARRATIVE PRESENCE (sense of having entered the story world), (d) EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT (sympathy/empathy/arousal toward characters). Transportation Scale (Green & Brock, 2000) — single higher-order absorption construct with cognitive/emotional/imagery facets (detailed in the sibling item). Related trait instruments referenced by the IES authors: Transportability (trait susceptibility to immersion), Narrative Engageability Scale (a separate multidimensional trait propensity-to-engage measure), and Parasociability.
cross_cultural_validity: Weakly established and implicitly Western/English-centric for all three instruments. The IES (2021) was developed and validated on English-speaking (predominantly US, online/undergraduate) samples; no cross-cultural measurement-invariance testing is reported in the development paper, and its constructs (coauthoring, reflection, individual theory-of-mind elaboration) reflect a Western individualist reading culture. The NEM (2009) has been very widely used and translated (e.g. German by Bilandzic and colleagues, and numerous other-language entertainment-education studies), and its overall reliability is robust (alpha ~.80+), but published systematic multigroup CFA / DIF invariance testing across languages is sparse; subscale internal consistencies are known to vary across stimuli and samples, which is itself a portability warning. Transportation (see sibling item) is similarly English/Western-dominant in its evidence base. What is likely cross-culturally robust: the broad existence of absorptive and reflective engagement. What likely does NOT transfer cleanly: the relative weighting of cognitive-elaboration subscales (gap-filling and theory-of-mind norms differ with reading/viewing traditions and with high- vs low-context communication cultures), and the calibration of self-report Likert engagement items. Treat any claim of invariance as unverified. [uncertain]
empirical_grounding: Strong for the family overall; this is the cluster's main strength and the reason it serves as a ground-truth target. Evidence is CORRELATIONAL and PSYCHOMETRIC (factor-analytic scale validation) plus some EXPERIMENTAL and PSYCHOPHYSIOLOGICAL corroboration. NEM (Busselle & Bilandzic, 2009): developed via factor analysis across multiple stories; all four subscales independently and significantly predict self-reported ENJOYMENT and story-related attitudes; later work (Bilandzic et al., embodiment study, 2016) tied NEM scores to real-time psychophysiological markers (heart rate, skin conductance, corrugator/facial activity), giving the self-report unusually good convergent validity. IES (Black et al., 2021): validated with factor analysis, showed discriminant validity from need for cognition and transportability, and demonstrated INCREMENTAL predictive validity — IES scores predicted story rating and state transportation beyond trait transportability. Transportation: meta-analytically supported (van Laer et al., 2014; see sibling item). What is needed to validate LLM-extracted dimensions against this framework: a corpus of works each scored by real viewers on NEM (state engagement) and/or rated for enjoyment, plus viewer IES/transportability trait scores, then a test of whether LLM-estimated synopsis-side antecedents (clear protagonist, emotional stakes, ambiguity/gaps that invite inference, character-psychology richness, thematic depth) predict the human engagement and enjoyment scores. Because engagement is a viewer×work outcome, only the work-side antecedents can be validated from text; the trait side (IES) cannot be derived from catalogue text at all.
operationalisability: Partly extractable, with a sharper cognitive caveat than transportation. The STATE/TRAIT scores themselves are NOT readable from a synopsis — NEM requires a viewer who watched the work; IES is a stable PERSON trait that must come from a user profile/history, never from catalogue text. What an LLM CAN estimate from a synopsis are the WORK-SIDE AFFORDANCES that invite each kind of engagement: for emotional engagement and presence — emotional stakes, a clear protagonist to follow, an immersive setting; for the IES cognitive subscales specifically — ambiguity, open endings, unreliable narration, and unstated motive (afford GAP-FILLING and COAUTHORING); psychologically complex, morally layered characters (afford THEORY OF MIND); thematic/philosophical density (affords REFLECTION). Extraction unit: WHOLE WORK for trait-priming affordances; ARC/EPISODE for serialised TV where some arcs are far more reflective or puzzle-like than others. Failure modes: (1) mistaking plot complexity for genuine ambiguity that invites gap-filling — a convoluted plot is not the same as a productive gap; (2) inability to see craft (direction, performance, editing) that actually drives ToM and presence but is invisible in a plot summary; (3) over-reading marketing copy engineered to sound 'thought-provoking'; (4) treating an affordance estimate as a work property when realised engagement is relational (a high-reflection film engages a high-IES viewer and bores a low-IES one); (5) conflating the affective and cognitive axes — a propulsive thriller can score high on absorption and low on reflection, and an LLM may collapse them.
motivation_served: Spans HEDONIC and EUDAIMONIC. The affective/absorptive subscales (presence, emotional engagement, attentional focus, transportation) map to HEDONIC enjoyment and MOOD REGULATION (pleasurable escape). The IES cognitive subscales — especially Reflection and Theory of Mind — map to EUDAIMONIC appreciation (meaning-making, being moved, working out a character or theme), and Coauthoring/Gap-Filling map to an active, playful, mastery-flavoured enjoyment (the pleasure of doing cognitive work with a story). For GROUP viewing: these instruments are valuable because they expose that two people can both 'enjoy' the same film for structurally different reasons — one transported and emotionally engaged, the other reflecting and theorising. A good joint pick offers BOTH affordances (emotional pull AND something to think about) so each partner's dominant engagement mode is fed, rather than forcing one viewer into the other's mode. The IES trait split (high-cognitive-engagement vs absorption-only viewers) is a candidate explanatory variable for why a 'demanding' arthouse pick lands for one partner and alienates the other.
receiver_dependence: Strongly RELATIONAL, and explicitly so — this is the cluster's central lesson for a two-person recommender. The IES is BY CONSTRUCTION a person trait (a habitual tendency), and Transportability is too; realised NEM/transportation engagement is a viewer×work×context outcome. No work has a fixed engagement value. The work contributes AFFORDANCES (extractable: emotional stakes, ambiguity/gaps, character psychological depth, thematic density) that raise the probability of each engagement type; whether they are taken up depends on the viewer's IES trait profile, transportability, need for cognition, mood, and familiarity. For HelpME2C this mandates a two-layer model: a work-side, synopsis-estimable 'engagement-affordance' layer (comparable across works) and a viewer-side trait layer (a per-user cognitive-vs-absorptive engagement profile, inferred from viewing history — NOT from catalogue text). The IES gives a principled vocabulary for that viewer-side layer. Conflating affordance with realised engagement would mislabel large relational variance as a property of the film.
blind_spots: (1) All are MODE/PROCESS measures, not CONTENT measures — they cannot on their own say which themes a person wants, only how a person tends to engage and how engaging an episode was. (2) Self-report and retrospective for NEM/transportation (remembered, not moment-to-moment, experience), though psychophysiological work partly mitigates this. (3) The IES, though it adds the cognitive axis, still under-theorises SOCIAL/ritual viewing (co-watching, fandom, second-screen), comfort re-watching where engagement is deliberately low, and ironic/critical distance. (4) Work-side affordances that drive engagement are largely craft-level (direction, performance, pacing) and mostly invisible in synopses, limiting computational use. (5) The instruments do not differentiate genres or themes — wildly different works can score identically. (6) Possible CULTURAL bias in the cognitive subscales (gap-filling/ToM norms vary across reading and communication cultures). (7) They measure engagement with a SINGLE viewer; none were designed for joint/dyadic viewing, the exact HelpME2C case.
vocabulary_implications: None of these are themes; do NOT add them as topical slugs. Instead they justify a small two-layer EXPERIENTIAL/COGNITIVE-AFFORDANCE facet, distinct from the topical theme vocabulary and complementary to the immersive-potential facet proposed in the sibling 'narrative-transportation' item. Proposed WORK-SIDE, synopsis-estimable affordance slugs/scores derived from the IES subscales: 'invites-gap-filling' (ambiguity, unstated motive, open ending — affords Gap-Filling/Coauthoring), 'character-psychology-rich' (complex, morally layered minds — affords Theory of Mind), 'thematically-reflective' (philosophical/thematic density — affords Reflection), plus the affective-side 'high-emotional-stakes' and 'immersive-world' already proposed for transportation. A coarse ordinal pair could summarise: 'cognitive-engagement-affordance' (low/med/high) and 'affective-engagement-affordance' (low/med/high). Flag explicitly as NOT extractable from synopsis and requiring other data sources: the actual NEM/state engagement scores (need viewer×work measurement); the viewer's IES TRAIT profile — cognitive vs absorptive engagement tendency (needs user viewing history, never catalogue text); and craft quality (needs reviews/ratings signals). Practical encoding: store affordances as work-side tags the LLM estimates from synopsis; keep a separate per-user 'engagement-style' trait layer (IES-flavoured) inferred from history; never collapse the two, and never collapse the cognitive and affective affordance axes into one number.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: Orthogonal to the topical/moral/identity slugs; operates one level up as experiential and cognitive modifiers, and reinforces the case made by the transportation item for promoting 'texture' to a separate facet. It VALIDATES texture slugs that already gesture at engagement MODE: 'character-study' and 'biographical-portrait' (high Theory-of-Mind and Reflection affordance), 'meditation-on-time'/'meditation-on-death' (high Reflection affordance), 'nonlinear-storytelling' (high Gap-Filling/Coauthoring affordance — the viewer must reconstruct order), 'slow-burn' (delayed payoff that rewards sustained cognitive and affective engagement), and 'mystery-investigation'/'whodunit' (Gap-Filling and Coauthoring as the core pleasure — the viewer races the narrative). It suggests these texture slugs are MISFILED alongside topical themes; they are really engagement-affordance descriptors. GAPS it reveals: HelpME2C has (a) no explicit cognitive-engagement / reflectiveness axis (the IES's distinctive contribution — there is no slug capturing 'this work makes you think and infer'), and (b) no viewer-side engagement-style trait layer at all, despite this cluster showing that cognitive-vs-absorptive engagement style is a large, stable individual difference that strongly predicts which works a person rates highly. Adding the cognitive-affordance facet plus a per-user engagement-style profile fills a hole that the transportation item's immersion axis alone does not cover.
key_sources: Black, J. E., Ruedinger, K., & Barnes, J. L. (2021). The Imaginative Engagement Scale: Development of an Instrument to Assess Cognitive Elements of Engaging with Fiction. Media Psychology, 25(4) — PRIMARY source for the Coauthoring/Gap-Filling/Theory-of-Mind/Reflection subscales. Free author PDF: https://jessicaeblack.org/research/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/Black-Ruedinger-Barnes-2021.pdf ; publisher (paywalled): https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15213269.2021.2012485 . Busselle, R., & Bilandzic, H. (2009). Measuring Narrative Engagement. Media Psychology, 12(4), 321-347 — the NEM and its four state dimensions. Free PDF (Augsburg OPUS): https://opus.bibliothek.uni-augsburg.de/opus4/files/39512/39512.pdf ; also http://hypermedia468.pbworks.com/w/file/fetch/80687372/measuring%20narrative%20engagement.pdf ; Semantic Scholar: https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Measuring-Narrative-Engagement-Busselle-Bilandzic/85d333e243752c2f345227aec903ff5ae4229310 . Bilandzic, H., et al. Embodiment of Narrative Engagement (Journal of Media Psychology, 28(4), 2016) — psychophysiological validation; https://econtent.hogrefe.com/doi/10.1027/1864-1105/a000153 (paywalled; preprint on ResearchGate). Narrative Engageability Scale (related trait measure): https://ijoc.org/index.php/ijoc/article/view/8624 (open access). Green, M. C., & Brock, T. C. (2000) Transportation Scale — see the sibling 'narrative-transportation' result for full treatment.
